channel.title: Target Test Prep on Arnesh's Website
channel.description: Recent content in Target Test Prep on Arnesh's Website

item.title: 1.1 Introduction
item.description: Lesson Content In the verbal section of the GMAT, between 9 and 13 of the 36 questions will be Sentence Correction questions. In this course, we will cover all of the concepts, strategies, and skills that you’ll need to correctly answer every Sentence Correction question you see on test day. Sentence Structure Subject-Verb Agreement Pronouns and Antecedents Modifiers Verb Tense, Mood, and Voice Parallelism Logical Comparisons Let’s get started on mastering Sentence Correction by discussing the basic layout of Sentence Correction questions.

item.title: 1.2 The Basic Layout of Sentence Correction Questions
item.description: Lesson Content Each Sentence Correction question presents us with a sentence, and all or part of the sentence is underlined. The first answer choice, answer choice (A), offers phrasing that is a repeat of the underlined portion of the original sentence. 💡MUST KNOW: In Sentence Correction questions, the first answer choice offers phrasing that is a repeat of underlined portion of the original sentence. Each of the other four choices presents an alternative way of wording the underlined portion of the sentence.

item.title: 1.3 GMAT Sentence Correction Questions Primarily Test Skill in Noticing Issues.
item.description: Lesson Content It would be easy to get the impression that Sentence Correction questions test only grammar knowledge, and the truth is that grammar errors do appear frequently in Sentence Correction questions. However, while grammar knowledge may serve as a good foundation for correctly answering Sentence Correction questions, such knowledge is neither sufficient to get us to the correct answer to many questions nor what Sentence Correction really tests. 💡MUST KNOW:

item.title: 1.4 When Answering Sentence Correction Questions, Read the Sentence Versions Holistically.
item.description: Lesson Content To consistently succeed in identifying issues in the sentence versions in Sentence Correction questions, we have to read the sentences in their entirety, or holistically. 💡MUST KNOW: To consistently succeed in identifying issues in the sentence versions in Sentence Correction questions, we have to read the sentences in their entirety, or holistically. If we consider the sentences only one part at a time, we can easily fool ourselves into thinking an incorrect version is correct.

item.title: 1.5 How To Approach Sentence Correction Questions
item.description: Lesson Content When answering a Sentence Correction question, begin by reading the entire original sentence to identify whether there are any clear errors of grammar or structure present. If the original version seems to be grammatically correct, then we keep choice (A) as a contender and move on to the other choices. 💡MUST KNOW: When we are answering a Sentence Correction question, if the original version seems to be grammatically correct, then we keep choice (A) as a contender and move on to the other choices.

item.title: 1.6 Do Not Give Sentence Correction Questions the Benefit of the Doubt: Read the Sentences Literally
item.description: Lesson Content When reading written communications (outside of Sentence Correction), we’re lenient with the writer and make efforts to understand the writer’s intended message, even if the writing has errors in grammar, word choice, style, or meaning. In other words, we give the writer the benefit of the doubt, saying “I know what the writer meant to say,” even if the writer didn’t exactly say what was meant. 💡MUST KNOW:

item.title: 1.7 The Non-Underlined Portion of the Sentence is just as Important as the Answer Choices.
item.description: Lesson Content Don’t for a second think that our job is to evaluate only the portion of the sentence that appears in the answer choices. In fact, often when a person falls into a Sentence Correction trap, she does so because she failed to carefully consider the non-underlined portion of the sentence. 💡MUST KNOW: When answering a Sentence Correction question, we must carefully consider both the underlined and the non-underlined portions of a sentence.

item.title: 1.8 Splits in Sentence Correction Questions
item.description: Lesson Content At some point, you may have heard of the idea of the Sentence Correction answer choices having “splits.” The idea of a split is simple. 💡MUST KNOW: We have a split when some of the answer choices utilize one structure while the other answer choices utilize a different, dueling structure. For example, maybe some choices use “their” while others use “its.” Maybe some choices use “because” while others use “due to.

item.title: 1.9 Looking for Strong Decision Points When Choosing Among Sentence Correction Answer Choices
item.description: Lesson Content In most incorrect answer choices in Sentence Correction questions, there are multiple flaws. 💡MUST KNOW: In most incorrect answer choices in Sentence Correction questions, there are multiple flaws. So, if we are not certain that one possible flaw is actually a flaw, we can generally find a flaw that we are more sure about. So, if we are having trouble deciding whether one characteristic of a sentence choice renders that choice incorrect, rather than spend time wondering about that one characteristic, we can look for a stronger or clearer reason to eliminate that choice.

item.title: 1.10 Seek Out Four Clearly Incorrect Answers, and Then Choose the One Remaining.
item.description: Lesson Content There are many ways in which sentences that appear in Sentence Correction questions can be correctly written. 💡MUST KNOW: Sometimes, even often, we won’t be used to recognizing the ways in which sentences that appear in Sentence Correction questions can be correctly written because the sentence structures are different from what we’re used to. Also, the correct versions of sentences in Sentence Correction questions are not always particularly well written.

item.title: 1.10.1 We Must Be Confident in Our Elimination of Incorrect Choices.
item.description: Lesson Content We must eliminate answer choices because we see why they are incorrect, not because we can’t see why they are correct. 💡MUST KNOW: In answering Sentence Correction questions, we must eliminate choices because we see why they are incorrect, not because we can’t see why they are correct. This brings up an important point. 💡MUST KNOW: As you move through the Target Test Prep Course, you must be able to articulate why each of the four incorrect answer choices is incorrect.

item.title: 1.10.2 What to Do If Stuck Between Two Final Answer Choices
item.description: Lesson Content Often, we’ll have correctly eliminated three of the five answer choices and be left with two. We’re seeking to determine what makes them different because both sentences can’t be correct, so there must be at least one key point of difference between them that will render one more effective (correct) than the other. Does one use the singular “is” while the other uses the plural verb “are?” Does one use the pronoun “their” while the other uses the singular “it?

item.title: 1.12 Don’t Eliminate an Answer Choice Because “It Changes the Meaning.”
item.description: Lesson Content Many people believe the false notion that the original, or choice (A), version of the sentence always conveys the meaning intended by the author. Let’s be clear about this: there is no such thing as “the intended meaning” in Sentence Correction questions. 💡MUST KNOW: There is no such thing as “the intended meaning” in Sentence Correction questions; there is nothing special about the meaning expressed by the version created via the use of choice (A).

item.title: 1.13 Don’t Eliminate an Answer Choice Because it Sounds “Awkward.”
item.description: Lesson Content Incorrect choices in Sentence Correction questions are incorrect because of clear errors in meaning, logic, or grammar. 💡MUST KNOW: Often, when a person does not see what’s wrong with an option in a Sentence Correction question, he labels the version as “awkward.” We’ve been teaching the GMAT for over 15 years, and to this day, we still haven’t seen anyone clearly articulate what the word “awkward” means about an incorrect sentence version.

item.title: 1.14 Do Not Seek to Determine What’s Correct or Incorrect Using Your “Ear.”
item.description: Lesson Content Many people approach sentence correction questions by using their “ears.” In other words, they choose answers based upon what “sounds” right to them or what feels right to them. 💡MUST KNOW: Approaching sentence correction questions by using their “ears” may help some people get some easy- and medium-level questions correct some of the time, but it certainly will not help most people get most of the questions – especially the harder questions – correct most of the time.

item.title: 1.15 Be Careful with Later Versions That Fix Earlier Errors.
item.description: Lesson Content Imagine that, in a question, the original sentence incorrectly uses the plural verb “were” with the singular subject “the company.” Most of you, even at this point, know that a singular subject must be paired with a singular verb. Now, what happens if choice (B) fixes this issue? In other words, what if the sentence produced by answer choice (B) correctly uses the singular noun “the company” with the singular verb “was.

item.title: 1.16 Be Careful of Falling Into Answer Choice (E).
item.description: Lesson Content Often, in answering Sentence Correction questions, people fall into choosing choice (E), the last answer choice, without a clear reason to choose choice (E). As you can imagine, falling into choosing choice (E) in this way often results in a person’s getting a question incorrect. To be clear, answer choice (E) will be correct just as frequently as the other four answer choices. However, don’t choose (E) just because you haven’t decided that another choice is correct, and (E) is the only one left.

item.title: 1.17 Timing and Sentence Correction Questions
item.description: Lesson Content On test day, each Sentence Correction question will take you, on average, about 1.5 to 1.75 minutes to complete. 💡MUST KNOW: On test day, each Sentence Correction question will take you about 1.5 to 1.75 minutes each to complete. Keep in mind that this time is an average; some will be solved faster, and some will require more time. That said, answering Sentence Correction questions when you are taking the GMAT and practicing answering Sentence Correction questions when you are preparing to take the GMAT are two different activities.

item.title: 1.18 We Can Leverage the Predictability of Sentence Correction Questions.
item.description: Lesson Content As you proceed, it will be helpful to keep in mind the fact that the errors that appear in Sentence Correction questions are limited to a handful of major topics. (1) Subject-Verb Agreement (2) Pronouns and Antecedents (3) Modifiers (4) Verb Tense, Mood, and Voice (5) Parallelism (6) Logical Comparisons (7) Sentence Structure (8) Idioms (9) Logic and Meaning Notice that, at a high level, there are not too many different types of errors that appear.

item.title: 2.1 Introduction
item.description: Lesson Content At the core of Sentence Correction is proper sentence structure, making this chapter one of the more important chapters in this course. Although the information presented here is foundational, almost all students will find either that they never learned this material or that much of it has been long forgotten. 💡MUST KNOW: Although the information presented here is foundational, almost all students will find either that they never learned this material or that much of it has been long forgotten.

item.title: 2.2 The Differences Between Phrases and Clauses
item.description: Lesson Content In language, we put words together to form two elementary types of structures: phrases and clauses. A phrase is simply a collection of words that cannot stand alone as a sentence because there is no subject-verb relationship. 💡MUST KNOW: A phrase is a collection of words without a subject-verb relationship. A phrase can never stand alone as a sentence. The following are examples of phrases: Notice that none of these phrases can stand alone as a sentence.

item.title: 2.3 Independent Clauses
item.description: Lesson Content There are two major hallmarks of an independent clause: · An independent clause contains both a subject and a finite verb. · An independent clause can stand alone as a sentence. 💡MUST KNOW: An independent clause contains a subject and a finite verb and can stand alone as a sentence. Why are independent clauses important? Well, every English sentence must have at least one independent clause, and the correct version of a sentence in a Sentence Correction question always contains at least one independent clause.

item.title: 2.4 Dependent Clauses
item.description: Lesson Content Clauses that cannot constitute sentences on their own are, quite logically, called dependent clauses. 💡MUST KNOW: Clauses that cannot constitute sentences on their own are called dependent clauses. These clauses fulfill specific functions within sentences and serve to enhance or modify the meaning expressed by an independent clause. There are three distinct types of dependent clauses: (1) Subordinate clauses (2) Relative clauses (3) Noun clauses Let’s continue by discussing subordinate clauses first.

item.title: 2.5 The Subordinate Clause
item.description: Lesson Content Consider one of our last examples of an independent clause: The crowd laughed. The clause has a subject and a finite verb - all that is needed, in this instance, for a complete sentence. Incorrect: Because the crowd laughed. Although you may not know exactly what is missing here, it’s almost certainly clear to you that we don’t have a sentence. “Because” is a member of a class of words called subordinating conjunctions, words that can take away the independence of a clause when placed at the head of the clause.

item.title: 2.6 What is a Sentence
item.description: Lesson Content In the simplest sense, a sentence is a collection of words that contains at least one independent clause. Let’s continue by talking about subjects. Most of you are familiar with the concept of the subject of a sentence. For example: Whales are highly intelligent animals. The blue ball was rolling down the hill. Doug will ask Martha to come to his house for dinner. In the sentences above, “whales,” “ball,” and “Doug” are the subjects performing the actions.

item.title: 2.7 Major Sentence Types Based on Structure
item.description: Lesson Content When talking about the structure of sentences, we divide sentences into four types: (1) Simple Sentences (2) Compound Sentences (3) Complex Sentences (4) Compound-Complex Sentences We’ll carefully discuss each of these sentence types, beginning with the simple sentence.

item.title: 2.8 The Simple Sentence
item.description: Lesson Content A simple sentence consists of a single independent clause followed by a period. 💡MUST KNOW: A simple sentence consists of a single independent clause followed by a period. The following are examples of simple sentences: I do. You will. I was at the beach. John and Jeff were reading books. Spending time with animals is one of the best ways to enjoy your days. We’ll discuss simple sentences in more detail as we progress, but for now it’s important to understand that the simple sentence is the simplest collection of words that can constitute an English sentence.

item.title: 2.9 The Compound Sentence
item.description: Lesson Content A compound sentence is composed of at least two independent clauses. 💡MUST KNOW: A compound sentence is composed of at least two independent clauses. We must connect these independent clauses in specific ways. One way is to use what’s referred to as a coordinating conjunction. There are seven coordinating conjunctions: for, and, nor, but, or, yet, so We can use the mnemonic device “FANBOYS” to help us distinguish these conjunctions from other types of transition words.

item.title: 2.10 A Semicolon Can be Used to Connect Two Independent Clauses
item.description: Lesson Content We have just discussed that one way to connect two independent clauses is by using a comma and a coordinating conjunction (FANBOYS). Consider the following example: IC IC My breakfast was cold; the door had been left open. The semicolon here acts in the same way as a comma and a coordinating conjunction would. When a semicolon is used as a substitute for a comma and coordinating conjunction, an independent clause must be found on each side of the semicolon.

item.title: 2.11 Correct Use of a Colon in a Sentence
item.description: Lesson Content We’ll encounter the colon from time to time in Sentence Correction questions. Whenever we have a colon, what precedes the colon must be able to stand on its own as a complete sentence. 💡MUST KNOW: Whenever we have a colon, what precedes the colon must be able to stand on its own as a complete sentence. For example: The board of directors made its final decision: the CEO will be replaced immediately.

item.title: 2.12 Complex Sentences
item.description: Lesson Content A complex sentence is composed of one independent clause and at least one subordinate clause (SC). 💡MUST KNOW: A complex sentence is composed of one independent clause and at least one subordinate clause. The following are examples of complex sentences: SC IC Because Lucy loves wine, I brought her a pinot noir from Burgundy. IC SC I brought Lucy a pinot noir from Burgundy because she loves wine.

item.title: 2.13 Compound-Complex Sentences
item.description: Lesson Content A compound-complex sentence is a sentence with more than one independent clause and at least one subordinate clause.For example: SC IC IC Because we went to the beach, I ran in the soft sand, so I could build my endurance. This example features one subordinate clause followed by two independent clauses, making this a compound-complex sentence. 💡MUST KNOW: A compound-complex sentence is a sentence with more than one independent clause and at least one subordinate clause.

item.title: 2.14 The Relative Clause: Another Type of Dependent Clause
item.description: Lesson Content Earlier in the chapter, we said that there are three types of dependent clauses: subordinate clauses, relative clauses, and noun clauses. Relative clauses begin with relative pronouns. that, which, who, whom, whose, where, when 💡MUST KNOW: Memorize the relative pronouns: A relative clause is a type of dependent clause, and as such, a relative clause is unable to stand alone as a sentence. Relative clauses always appear embedded within other clauses.

item.title: 2.15 Relative Clauses are Often Used to Create Sentence Fragments.
item.description: Lesson Content Remember that a relative clause is a dependent clause, and therefore it cannot stand alone as a sentence. 💡MUST KNOW: A relative clause is a dependent clause, and therefore it cannot stand alone as a sentence. In other words, a relative clause must be combined with an independent clause if we are to have a sentence. However, in Sentence Correction questions, we’ll often encounter sentence versions that are just relative clauses attached to nouns.

item.title: 2.16 Noun Clauses
item.description: Lesson Content A noun clause is a clause that plays the role of a noun. Many noun clauses begin with the following words: that, which, how, who, whom, where, whether, what, why For example, in each of the following sentences the noun clauses are bolded: Marty feels that spiritual development has no limits. That holiday leave had been cancelled depressed the entire company. The crowd was unhappy with what happened next.

item.title: 2.17 A Noun Clause Can Serve as the Subject of a Sentence.
item.description: Lesson Content Many people believe that the subject of a sentence must be a noun. That Napoleon’s ruse would fail in its intended objective had not occurred to the French generals. This is a perfectly acceptable sentence. Notice that each of the following sentences has a noun clause serving as the subject: That she had the nerve to attend the party is shocking. What few believed could exist was eventually created.

item.title: 2.18 A Noun Clause Can Serve as a Direct Object
item.description: Lesson Content We already know that a noun clause can serve as the subject of a sentence. For example: Dayo picked apples. The noun “apples” is the direct object. For example: The deer ate the green grass. The noun phrase “the green grass” is the direct object. Direct objects don’t have to be single nouns though. I knew why the deer was eating my flowers. I felt that it was time to go swimming.

item.title: 2.19 Noun Clause as Object of a Preposition
item.description: Lesson Content A noun clause can also serve as the object of a preposition. Incorrect: I was unhappy about. Incorrect: The bill was limited by. Those sets of words do not express complete thoughts. For example, a preposition can be followed by a single noun: I was unhappy about the weather. I was unhappy about the oranges. I was unhappy about market conditions. The bill was limited by greed. The bill was limited by time.

item.title: 2.20 Distinguishing Between Relative Clauses and Noun Clauses
item.description: Lesson Content The proper use of relative clauses and noun clauses is important in GMAT Sentence Correction. Since relative clauses and noun clauses look similar, when we see a clause that begins with a word such as “that,” “which,” “who,” or “where,” we need to decide whether it is a relative clause or a noun clause. It will help to remember that a relative clause must follow the noun that the clause is modifying.

item.title: 2.21 Items in a Series
item.description: Lesson Content When three or more items appear in a series, a comma and a coordinating conjunction must appear before the last item in the series. For breakfast, I had cereal, toast, and juice. Notice that, in this sentence, a comma and the coordinating conjunction “and” precede the final noun, “juice.” The comma just before the coordinating conjunction is referred to as the “Oxford Comma.” It must be present in such a list on the GMAT.

item.title: 2.22 Using Semicolons to Clarify Lists
item.description: Lesson Content As we know, a semicolon can be used between two independent clauses. Consider the following example: When Monica is in Europe, she’ll visit London, England; Paris, France; Rome, Italy; and Vienna, Austria. Notice that the sentence uses semicolons to more clearly separate items in a list that could be confusing if commas were used instead. Imagine the sentence without the semicolons: Incorrect: When Monica is in Europe, she’ll visit London, England, Paris, France, Rome, Italy, and Vienna, Austria.

item.title: 3.1 Introduction
item.description: Lesson Content At this point, we already know that any English sentence must contain at least one subject and at least one finite verb serving as the main verb of the sentence. 💡MUST KNOW: Any English sentence must contain at least one subject and at least one finite verb serving as the main verb of the sentence. We also know that finite verbs are special in the sense that they allow us to determine when in time a subject performs an action.

item.title: 3.2 A Subject and Its Finite Verb in Any Clause Must Agree in Number
item.description: Lesson Content In addition to the rule that each sentence must have at least one subject and at least one main verb, when a sentence has more than one clause, each of the clauses must contain a subject that agrees in number with the finite verb in that clause. In other words, if the subject of a clause is singular, the subject’s finite verb in that clause must be singular, and if the subject of a clause is plural, the subject’s finite verb in that clause must be plural.

item.title: 3.3 Identifying the Subject and Finite Verb in a Clause
item.description: Lesson Content In order to ensure that the subject and finite verb of a clause agree, we must first identify these two parts of the clause. 💡MUST KNOW: The subject of a clause is the noun that is doing or being what the main verb expresses. In order to determine which noun is doing or being what the finite verb expresses, and is thus the subject, first identify the action word in the clause, which is the main verb.

item.title: 3.4 Placing A Prepositional Phrase Between the Subject and Its Verb
item.description: Lesson Content Our ears have been trained to pair verbs with the nouns immediately preceding them, so we may make a mistake when a prepositional phrase separates a subject and its verb. 💡MUST KNOW: Our ears have been trained to pair verbs with the nouns immediately preceding them, so we may make a mistake when a prepositional phrase separates a subject and its verb. As a quick refresher, remember that a prepositional phrase is a type of modifying phrase that begins with a preposition, such as “by,” “for,” or “in.

item.title: 3.5 Placing an Appositive Between the Subject and Its Verb
item.description: Lesson Content An appositive is an element of a sentence that modifies or further describes another element of a sentence by renaming it. My brother John loves hiking in the Ozark mountains. In the above example, the noun “brother” is a general noun. Let’s consider another example that uses an appositive: The uninvited guest, a large spider, is creating a web above my television. In this example, “a large spider” is an appositive that further describes the noun “uninvited guest.

item.title: 3.6 Placing a Relative Clause Between a Subject and its Verb
item.description: Lesson Content We already know that a relative clause is a clause that begins with a relative pronoun, such as “that,” “which,” “when,” “where,” “who,” or “whom.” The following are examples of relative clauses: that will be fun for everyone which is the longest river in Asia who is the nicest woman we know where the black rhinoceros prefers to sleep when the music stops playing A relative clause can be placed between a subject and its finite verb to make catching subject-verb agreement issues more challenging.

item.title: 3.7 Compound Subjects
item.description: Lesson Content A compound subject is a subject composed of multiple nouns joined by the coordinating conjunction “and” or “or.” The following examples have compound subjects (bolded): The sidewalk and the street were damaged by the earthquake. The largest of the existing land animals, elephants and rhinoceroses are herbivores. 💡MUST KNOW: A compound subject is a subject composed of multiple nouns joined by the coordinating conjunction “and” or “or.” Sometimes compound subjects require plural verbs, but other times they do not.

item.title: 3.8 Additive Phrases Don’t Create Plural Subjects
item.description: Lesson Content In the last section, we saw that the use of the word “and” can result in the creation of a compound plural subject that requires a plural verb. In this section, we’re going to discuss additive phrases. What we’ll see as we discuss additive phrases is that, even though expressions such as “along with” or “as well as” resemble “and,” additive phrases don’t work the way “and” works with sentence subjects.

item.title: 3.9 Or, Either … Or, and Neither … Nor
item.description: Lesson Content Sentences and clauses containing compound subjects joined by the conjunctions “or,” “either … or,” or “neither … nor” abide by their own special subject-verb agreement rule. In these cases, the verb must agree with the subject noun that is closest to the verb. 💡MUST KNOW: The verb in a clause or sentence containing a compound subject joined by the conjunctions “or,” “either … or,” or “neither … nor” must agree with the subject noun to which it is closest.

item.title: 3.10 Inverted Sentences
item.description: Lesson Content Leave it to the GMAT test-makers to turn sentences inside out. 💡MUST KNOW: In an inverted sentence, the main verb precedes the subject. Let’s look at some examples of inverted sentences. The first version of the sentence below is the version that most of us would write; we tend to place the subject before its finite verb. Coyotes,medium-sized, dog-like animals, are found in many suburban areas. Found in many suburban areas are coyotes, medium-sized, dog-like animals.

item.title: 3.11 Collective Nouns
item.description: Lesson Content Collective nouns are nouns that name groups of people or things. 💡MUST KNOW: Collective nouns are nouns that refer to groups of people or things. When collective nouns appear as subjects on GMAT Sentence Correction questions, they are almost always considered singular. Consider the following sentence: The team is at the lake this weekend, for a workshop on conflict resolution skills. Notice that the collective noun “team” is singular since in this context it refers to a single collection of people.

item.title: 3.12 The Collective Nouns “Majority” and “Minority”
item.description: Lesson Content Like other collective nouns that we have discussed, the collective nouns “majority” and “minority” can be considered singular or plural depending on the contexts in which they appear. In the following sentences, “majority” and “minority” are plural: A majority of the students are already applying for jobs. In today’s world, in which most people have some knowledge of geography, only a small minority believe Earth to be flat.

item.title: 3.13 Collective Nouns that are not Clearly Singular or Plural in Sentence Correction Questions
item.description: Lesson Content Of course, since a collective noun subject can be correctly paired with a singular verb or plural verb, we could see a Sentence Correction question in which it is not entirely clear whether a collective noun would most logically be considered a singular subject or plural subject. In such a case, our best move may be to choose an answer choice by looking for differences between choices other than agreement between that collective noun and its associated verb.

item.title: 3.14 Subjects with Certain Preceding Elements
item.description: Lesson Content Some preceding elements in a subject can require that the subject take a singular verb even if the subject seems to be plural. For instance, a subject composed of a noun preceded by “every,” “each,” or “many a” always takes a singular verb. Consider the following examples: The guys at the prom look amazing. Every guy at the prom looks amazing. In the first sentence, the subject, “guys,” is plural and therefore requires a plural verb.

item.title: 3.15 Pronoun-Verb Agreement
item.description: Lesson Content A pronoun is a word that takes the place of a noun. Robert said that he will be able to attend the party. In this sentence, the pronoun “he” refers to the antecedent “Robert.” Notice that, by using the pronoun, we don’t have to repeat the noun “Robert.” The zebra and the hippo decided that they were tired, so they looked for a comfortable place to sleep. In this sentence, the pronoun “they” refers to the antecedents “the zebra and the hippo.

item.title: 3.16 Relative Pronouns
item.description: Lesson Content Relative pronouns, such as “which,” “that,” and “who,” can be singular or plural depending on their referents. The dresser that is on sale is still expensive. The curtains that are on sale are beautiful. As demonstrated in the sentences above, the relative pronoun “that” can take a singular or plural verb depending on whether the noun to which “that” refers is singular or plural. 💡MUST KNOW: When a relative pronoun refers to a singular noun, it too is singular and takes a singular verb.

item.title: 3.17 Indefinite Pronouns
item.description: Lesson Content An indefinite pronoun is exactly what it sounds like, a pronoun that doesn’t refer to any one thing in particular. 💡MUST KNOW: An indefinite pronoun is a pronoun that doesn’t refer to any one thing in particular. There are many indefinite pronouns, and subject-verb agreement rules vary depending on which one is used. Let’s first discuss singular indefinite pronouns.

item.title: 3.18 Singular Indefinite Pronouns
item.description: Lesson Content Some indefinite pronouns are always singular and, therefore, always require a singular verb. These pronouns are: anybody, anyone, anything, each, either1, everybody, everyone, everything, neither1, nobody, no one, nothing, one, somebody, someone, something, whatever, whoever2 Consider the following examples in which singular indefinite pronouns are paired with singular verbs: After the concert, everyone was ready to go home. If someone fixes up this house, it will be lovely.

item.title: 3.19 Plural Indefinite Pronouns
item.description: Lesson Content Some indefinite pronouns are always plural and therefore always take a plural verb. These indefinite pronouns include the following: “both,” “few,” “many,” and “several.” Be sure to memorize these plural indefinite pronouns. 💡MUST KNOW: A plural indefinite pronoun always takes a plural verb. These pronouns include the following: “both,” “few,” “many,” and “several.” In most cases, it is intuitive to pair a plural verb with a plural indefinite pronoun.

item.title: 3.20 Other Indefinite Pronouns – Sometimes Singular, Sometimes Plural
item.description: Lesson Content Some indefinite pronouns can be singular or plural depending on their referents. Such indefinite pronouns include “some,” “any,” “none,” “all,” “more,” and “most.” You can remember this list by using the acronym SANAMM, which consists of the first letters of the pronouns in the list, “Some,” “Any,” “None,” “All,” “More,” and “Most.” These indefinite pronouns are often referred to as the SANAMM pronouns. The rules of usage of these pronouns are simple:

item.title: 3.21 Subject-Verb Agreement Involving Numbers, Fractions, and Percentages
item.description: Lesson Content Let’s begin our discussion of subject-verb agreement involving numbers, fractions, percentages by discussing subjects that include the word “number.” In a sentence subject, the word “number” can be preceded by either “the” or “a.” When, in a subject, “number” is preceded by “the,” the sentence is about the number itself. The number of students who apply for summer internships has been increasing. An official count revealed that the number of people who attended the show was 3521.

item.title: 3.22 Subject-Verb Agreement Involving the Word “Percentage”
item.description: Lesson Content Like “number,” “percentage,” in a sentence subject, can be preceded by either “the” or “a.” When, in a subject, “percentage” is preceded by “the,” the sentence is about the percentage itself, and so the subject is singular, as are the subjects in the following examples. The percentage of people in America who do note vote is astounding. Most experts believed that the percentage of cars represented by the electric car category was going to increase rapidly.

item.title: 3.23 Subject-Verb Agreement Involving Fractions
item.description: Lesson Content We have discussed that subjects that begin with the SANAMM pronouns can be singular or plural depending on the nouns to which the pronouns refer. Consider the following examples: Half of the class is going on a field trip. One-third of the students have shown some interest in geology. In the first sentence, the fraction “half” is part of a single entity, “the class.” Therefore, the subject, “half of the class,” takes a singular verb, “is.

item.title: 3.24 Subject-Verb Agreement and the Word “Percent”
item.description: Lesson Content The rules we follow in situations in which portions are described via the use of the word “percent” are similar to the ones we follow in situations in which subjects involve fractions. Consider the following examples: Ninety percent of the board was present. Fifty percent of the board members are in favor of the acquisition. Notice that the first sentence is about “ninety percent” of “the board.” Since “the board” is singular, “ninety percent of the board” agrees with the singular verb “was.

item.title: 3.25 Unusual Singular and Plural Forms
item.description: Lesson Content In English, nouns that end in -s are often plural. 1 Datum/Data: While “datum” and “data” are, strictly speaking, the singular and plural of “datum,” usage has evolved, and currently, “data” can be used to convey a singular meaning or a plural meaning. 2 Medium/Media: When “media” refers to the press, it is often considered a collective noun that takes a singular verb (e.g., “The media is out in full force”).

item.title: 3.26 Nontraditional Subjects
item.description: Lesson Content In some cases, the subjects of sentences aren’t simple nouns. Let’s take a look at some examples: To catch trains is difficult at certain times of the day. Swimming is good exercise. What I want is for you to be healthy. In the first sentence, the subject is “to catch trains,” which is an infinitive. In the second sentence, the subject is “Swimming,” a gerund, which is a verb acting as a noun and ending in -ing.

item.title: 4.1 Introduction
item.description: Lesson Content As we already know, a pronoun is a word that takes the place of a noun. 💡MUST KNOW: A pronoun is a word that takes the place of a noun. We use pronouns so that we don’t have to repeat nouns in sentences. For example: Sara is a skilled artist, and she consistently produces intriguing work. The pronoun “she” takes the place of the noun “Sara.” We refer, then, to “Sara” as the antecedent (or the referent) of the pronoun “she.

item.title: 4.2 Pronoun Issue One: Multiple Possible Antecedents
item.description: Lesson Content As we now know, an antecedent, or referent, is the noun to which a pronoun refers. 💡MUST KNOW: An antecedent, or referent, is the noun to which a pronoun refers. A pronoun can refer to a noun that comes either before or after it in a sentence. Of course, in order for a sentence to effectively convey a meaning, it must be clear which noun is the antecedent of each pronoun in the sentence.

item.title: 4.2.1 Meaning Can Indicate the Referent of a Pronoun.
item.description: Lesson Content Sometimes, even when there are multiple nouns to which a pronoun in a sentence could conceivably refer, which noun is the referent of the pronoun may be clear because meaning indicates which noun the pronoun refers to. 💡MUST KNOW: Sometimes, even when there are multiple nouns to which a pronoun in a sentence could conceivably refer, which noun is the referent of the pronoun may be clear because meaning indicates which noun the pronoun refers to.

item.title: 4.3 Pronoun Issue Two: A Pronoun’s Antecedent Is Missing from the Sentence
item.description: Lesson Content Whereas some sentences will feature a pronoun with multiple possible antecedents, as we saw in the previous section, others will lack an antecedent altogether. Of course, as we know, each pronoun in a sentence must have one clear antecedent. 💡MUST KNOW: We may encounter sentences that feature a pronoun but lack an antecedent for that pronoun. Answer choices offering such sentences will be incorrect. Consider the following example:

item.title: 4.4 Pronoun Issue Three: A Pronoun has an Adjective as its Antecedent.
item.description: Lesson Content Pronouns cannot take adjectives as their antecedents. 💡MUST KNOW: An adjective can’t be the antecedent of a pronoun. Sometimes in a sentence, a word that could serve as the antecedent of a pronoun is present but acting, not as a noun, but as an adjective and, therefore, cannot function logically as the antecedent of a pronoun in that sentence. Consider the following example: Incorrect: The number of grams of Colombian gold sold annually has increased each year since 2005, a fact demonstrating buyer confidence in the quality of the gold produced there.

item.title: 4.4.1 It’s Acceptable to Have a Possessive as the Antecedent of a Pronoun.
item.description: Lesson Content Unlike many other authorities on English grammar, which do not consider it acceptable for a noun in the possessive form to be the antecedent of a pronoun, the GMAT does consider it acceptable for a noun in the possessive form to be the antecedent of a pronoun. 💡MUST KNOW: The GMAT considers it acceptable for a noun in the possessive form to be the antecedent of a pronoun.

item.title: 4.5 Pronoun Issue Four: A Pronoun Doesn’t Agree in Number with its Antecedent.
item.description: Lesson Content Just as subjects must agree in number with their verbs, pronouns and antecedents must agree. 💡MUST KNOW: A pronoun must agree in number with its antecedent. A singular pronoun must refer to a singular antecedent, and a plural pronoun must refer to a plural antecedent. To ensure pronoun-antecedent agreement, when there is a pronoun in a sentence, identify its antecedent and check for agreement. Incorrect: The prices at the department store are set high, and it is marked down to normal levels on sale days.

item.title: 4.5.1 Pronouns that Refer to “Each” or “Every” Must Be Singular.
item.description: Lesson Content Pronouns that refer to “each” or “every” must be singular, regardless of whether “each” or “every” appears at the beginning of a phrase that contains a plural noun or that describes a group. 💡MUST KNOW: Pronouns that correctly refer to “each” or “every” are always singular, regardless of whether “each” or “every” begins a phrase that contains a plural noun or that describes a group. Consider the following sentences:

item.title: 4.5.2 Pronoun-Antecedent Agreement when the Antecedent is an Indefinite Pronoun
item.description: Lesson Content As we already know, indefinite pronouns are pronouns that do not refer to a specific person, place, or thing. Singular Indefinite Pronouns Plural Indefinite Pronouns Other Indefinite Pronouns – Sometimes Singular, Sometimes Plural Some of the most common pronoun-antecedent errors on the GMAT include two pronouns – an indefinite pronoun and a possessive pronoun. Take the following example: Incorrect: Everyone has their own problems. In this sentence, we have two pronouns: the indefinite pronoun “everyone” and the possessive pronoun “their.

item.title: 4.6 Pronoun Issue Five: A Sentence Contains an Unneeded Pronoun.
item.description: Lesson Content Some sentences contain unnecessary pronouns. 💡MUST KNOW: If a sentence contains a noun and a pronoun that play the same role or two pronouns that play the same role, then the sentence contains a useless word and, thus, is illogically constructed. Consider the following example: Incorrect: Rich Roll, once an unhealthy corporate lawyer, he decided to change his life at age 40. While the above sentence may sound basically fine, if we consider it carefully, we can see that the noun “Rich Roll” and the pronoun “he” both fill the role of the subject of the sentence.

item.title: 4.7 Pronoun Issue Six: The Sentence Contains Illogical Antecedents.
item.description: Lesson Content Some pronoun-antecedent errors on the GMAT involve pronouns and antecedents that don’t work logically with each other. Incorrect: The term “social media” is often used to describe platforms such as Facebook and Twitter, but it is actually any form of internet communication, including blogs, wikis, and more. In this sentence, the pronoun “it” refers to the antecedent ‘the term “social media,”’ not “social media” itself. The term “social media” is often used to describe platforms such as Facebook and Twitter, but it actually refers to any form of internet communication, including blogs, wikis, and more.

item.title: 4.8 Pronoun Issue Seven: A Pronoun is Referring to a Clause.
item.description: Lesson Content Although, in everyday speech, people often use pronouns to refer to clauses, on the GMAT, constructions that involve pronouns referring to clauses are not correct. 💡MUST KNOW: On the GMAT, versions in which pronouns refer to entire clauses are not correct. Consider the following sentence: Incorrect: My dog remained lost for days, which caused me to lose sleep until it was found. As currently structured, the relative pronoun “which” appears to refer to the entire clause “My dog was lost for days.

item.title: 4.9 Pronoun Issue Eight: The Sentence Uses an Incorrect Pronoun Case.
item.description: Lesson Content A pronoun’s case is its categorization into one of three types: subject, object, and possessive. To see how pronouns of these types function in sentences, consider the following examples: I invited you to our Halloween party. “I” is the subject pronoun. Everybody is hoping that the star player stays on his current team. “Everybody” is the subject pronoun because it is doing the action of hoping. You should give your mother the flowers that they picked.

item.title: 4.9.1 Errors in Pronoun Case in Compound Subjects and Compound Objects
item.description: Lesson Content Sometimes we see a pronoun as part of a compound subject or compound object. Jack and I went snorkeling in Maui. Would you like to eat dinner with Sara and me? If neither he nor the director attends the event, we’ll use stock photographs of him and the other cast members. Of course, in a Sentence Correction question, a pronoun in a compound subject or compound object could be of the wrong case.

item.title: 4.9.2 “Who”/“Whoever” and “Whom”/“Whomever”
item.description: Lesson Content In the list at the beginning of this section, we saw that “who” and “whoever” are subject pronouns and that “whom” and “whomever” are object pronouns. While which of the pronouns are which case is not difficult to remember, which of them should be used in a particular situation is not always obvious, and of course, GMAT question writers can use “who”/“whoever” or “whom”/“whomever” in creating tricky Sentence Correction answer choices.

item.title: 4.9.3 Possessive Pronouns and Gerunds
item.description: Lesson Content A gerund is a form of verb that ends in “-ing” and is used as a noun. Skateboarding is popular worldwide. Many people enjoy public speaking. Often, in sentences, gerunds are preceded by possessive pronouns. Consider the following example: During the meeting with the citizens, the mayor seemed to be surprised by their questioning the validity of the data. While you might be tempted to believe that the correct wording is “surprised by them questioning,” the sentence is actually correct as written, with the possessive pronoun “their” preceding the gerund “questioning.

item.title: 4.10 Demonstrative Pronouns and Demonstrative Adjectives
item.description: Lesson Content A demonstrative pronoun is a pronoun that points out or “demonstrates” the noun in the sentence. 💡MUST KNOW: A demonstrative pronoun takes the place of a noun and always stands alone. The major demonstrative pronouns are “this,” “that,” “these,” and “those.” Consider the following example: The tires on most sports cars are wider than those on most mid-size sedans. In the sentence above, “those” is a demonstrative pronoun.

item.title: 4.11 The Expletive “It” and Delayed Subjects
item.description: Lesson Content As we look out for pronouns without logical referents, we have to be careful not to be confused by what is called “the expletive ‘it.’” Consider the following examples: It is apparent that Milan is a skilled writer. Because he had been training, it was easy for Michael to swim across the lake. In each of the above examples, the pronoun “it” has no referent and thus may seem to be incorrectly used.

item.title: 5.1 Introduction
item.description: Lesson Content A modifier is a word, phrase, or clause that modifies, or changes, another word or expression. 💡MUST KNOW: A modifier is a word, phrase, or clause that modifies, or changes, another word or expression. As you already know, in answering SC questions, the evaluation of a sentence’s meaning and logic is just as important as the evaluation of a sentence’s grammar and style. The GMAT uses SC questions involving modifiers to test our skill in determining what makes sense and what doesn’t.

item.title: 5.2 Adjectives and Adverbs
item.description: Lesson Content Adjectives are modifiers that can modify only nouns or pronouns, while adverbs are modifiers that can modify verbs, adjectives, and other adverbs. 💡MUST KNOW: Adjectives can modify only nouns or pronouns. Adverbs can modify verbs, adjectives, and other adverbs. Here are some examples of common adjectives: hard, bright, pretty, thoughtless, spurious, enthusiastic, yellow, tentative, sonorous, new, good. The new book is now in stores. She made a thoughtless comment.

item.title: 5.2.1 Using an Adjective where an Adverb is Required or an Adverb where an Adjective is Required
item.description: Lesson Content One way in which adjectives and adverbs can be used to create issues in Sentence Correction questions is via using an adverb when an adjective is required or vice versa. How do we know whether we need an adjective or an adverb in a sentence? The context will always tell us whether an adjective or adverb is correct. 💡MUST KNOW: The meaning of a sentence will enable us to determine whether an adverb or an adjective should be used at a given point in a sentence.

item.title: 5.3 Misplaced Modifiers
item.description: Lesson Content As the name implies, a misplaced modifier is a modifier that is in the wrong place in a sentence. 💡MUST KNOW: The misplacement of modifiers results in errors in meaning, and misplaced modifiers are frequently used in incorrect answer choices in Sentence Correction questions. Misplaced modifier questions can be particularly challenging because even native English speakers often misplace modifiers in their speech and writing. Let’s see how misplacement can affect the meaning of a sentence by considering the effects of misplacing adjectives.

item.title: 5.3.1 Misplaced Adjectives
item.description: Lesson Content When an adjective is incorrectly separated from the noun it modifies, the meaning of the sentence can be distorted. Incorrect: On the tables at the event were nutritious trays of fruits and vegetables. Hmm – those nutritious trays sure sound delicious, don’t they? Better have a good dentist on call, right? Of course, the adjective “nutritious” is misplaced. On the tables at the event were trays of nutritious fruits and vegetables.

item.title: 5.3.2 Misplaced Adverbs
item.description: Lesson Content When we evaluate a sentence, we must take care to ensure that any adverbs that the sentence contains are placed logically since, like the incorrect placement of an adjective, the incorrect placement of an adverb can significantly distort the meaning of a sentence. Although we could use any adverbs as examples, for this discussion, let’s focus on some adverbs that are called limiting words, since these words frequently appear in misplaced modifier questions.

item.title: 5.3.3 A Noun Modifier Must Be Placed as Closely as Possible to What It Modifies.
item.description: Lesson Content As you’ve begun to see, the positions of words, phrases, and clauses matter greatly to the meaning of a sentence, and small changes in the placement of words, phrases, and clauses can translate into dramatic changes in the meaning of a sentence. 💡MUST KNOW: A noun modifier must be placed as closely as possible to what it modifies. Consider the following examples in which the modifiers are in bold and the nouns being modified are underlined.

item.title: 5.3.4 Misplaced Prepositional Phrases
item.description: Lesson Content A prepositional phrase is a modifier composed of a preposition, such as “in,” “under,” “by,” or “for,” followed by a noun, and possibly some modifiers that modify that noun. in boxes under rocks by the lake for illiterate individuals Prepositional phrases can serve as adjectives or adverbs. When prepositional phrases serve as adjectives, they modify nouns, as in the following examples: On the shelf was chocolate in boxes.

item.title: 5.3.5 Pay Special Attention to Prepositional Phrases of Time
item.description: Lesson Content Prepositional phrases of time include structures such as “by 1920,” “after the Cold War ended,” and “in 2018.” When we encounter such phrases, we must take extra care to ensure that their placement does not result in ambiguity, confusion, or an illogical meaning. Incorrect: The board of directors said in 2020 the price of the company’s product would need to double. This sentence is confusing at best. The board of directors said the price of the company’s product would need to double in 2020.

item.title: 5.3.6 Misplaced Introductory Modifiers
item.description: Lesson Content We often encounter sentences that open with introductory (opening) modifiers. Consider the following example: Built to last, the lighthouse has weathered many storms. We can see that the sentence opens with an introductory noun modifier, “built to last.” The noun that the introductory modifier is meant to modify, “the lighthouse,” appears right after the introductory modifier. 💡MUST KNOW: When an introductory modifier is a noun modifier, the noun that it modifies must appear right after the introductory modifier.

item.title: 5.4 Dangling Modifiers
item.description: Lesson Content Dangling modifiers are similar to misplaced modifiers, except in that, in a case of a dangling modifier, the noun that the modifier is supposed to describe is completely absent from the sentence. 💡MUST KNOW: In a case of a dangling modifier, the noun that the modifier is supposed to describe is completely absent from the sentence. Such a sentence is always incorrect. Dangling modifiers are common in Sentence Correction questions.

item.title: 5.5 The Possessive Modification Trap
item.description: Lesson Content Nouns in possessive form often don’t seem much different from ordinary nouns, but the two types of nouns do not work in the same way in sentences. Consider the following sentence: Incorrect: Awarded the Nobel Prize for his work in chemistry, Linus Pauling’s research was wide-ranging and hard-hitting, and had a positive impact on many future generations of chemists. The possessive “Linus Pauling’s” is not the same as the standalone noun “Linus Pauling.

item.title: 5.6 Squinting Modifiers
item.description: Lesson Content When a modifier is placed between two words or expressions, either of which it could modify, the conveyed meaning can be ambiguous. Incorrect: People who train consistently achieve score increases. Notice that the adverb “consistently” is a squinting modifier because it is placed between two verbs, “train” and “achieve,” either of which it could be modifying. For the sentence to have one clear meaning, the modifier must be placed differently, so that it becomes clear which word is being modified.

item.title: 5.7 Relative Clauses
item.description: Lesson Content

item.title: 5.7.1 “That” versus “Which”
item.description: Lesson Content The relative pronouns “that” and “which” refer to nouns that represent things other than people. 💡MUST KNOW: The relative pronouns “that” and “which” refer to nouns that represent things other than people. In other words, never use “that” or “which” to refer to people. Because both “that” and “which” refer to objects or ideas (but never to people), they are easily confused. All the same, in Sentence Correction questions, the following common convention has been followed.

item.title: 5.7.2 The Proper Placement of Relative Clauses
item.description: Lesson Content Like faulty placement of adjectives and adverbs, misplacement of relative clauses can result in the creation of illogical sentences. Incorrect: The salesman sold the car to the woman that had 15,000 miles. Notice that the restrictive relative clause “that had 15,000 miles” is incorrectly modifying “woman.” Of course, it’s not the woman who had 15,000 miles; it’s the car she purchased. The salesman sold to the woman the car that had 15,000 miles.

item.title: 5.7.3 When Relative Clauses Can Modify Their Antecedents “Remotely”
item.description: Lesson Content In most cases in which we see a relative clause in a correct answer to a Sentence Correction question, that clause will directly follow the noun that the clause modifies. 💡MUST KNOW: There are structures that are considered acceptable or correct in which a relative clause is separated from the noun that it modifies. In such a structure, the relative clause is called a “remote relative clause.” There are two ways in which a relative clause may be separated from the noun that it modifies in a correct answer.

item.title: 5.7.4 “That,” “Who,” “Whom,” and “Which”
item.description: Lesson Content As we discussed in a previous section, the relative pronoun “that” can be correctly used to introduce a restrictive relative clause. In formal writing, the relative pronoun “that” should refer only to objects or ideas, never people. For example: The woman who lives next door to me is a pianist. A woman is a “who.” Incorrect: The man that plays the violin also has a very good voice.

item.title: 5.7.5 “Who” and “Whom”
item.description: Lesson Content We should also be clear about the correct usage of “who” and “whom.” As discussed in the Pronouns chapter, the pronouns “who” and “whom” should not be used interchangeably, even though both refer to people, not objects. A subject pronoun performs an action, whereas an object pronoun is acted upon. 💡MUST KNOW: The subject pronoun “who” performs the action in a clause, whereas the object pronoun “whom” is acted upon.

item.title: 5.7.6 Whose
item.description: Lesson Content The relative pronoun “whose” can refer to things or people. The car whose fender I dented belongs to the police chief. My mother, whose bracelet I borrowed, has always been willing to share. The dog whose paws are big also has a loving heart. 💡MUST KNOW: The relative pronoun “whose” can refer to things or people. “Whose” is the English language’s only possessive relative pronoun. Let’s do some Targeted Practice.

item.title: 5.8 Appositives
item.description: Lesson Content An appositive is an element of a sentence that modifies or further describes another element of a sentence by renaming it. On the GMAT, appositives generally appear in the forms of nouns, pronouns, noun phrases, or noun clauses that are used to modify other nouns, pronouns, noun phrases, or noun clauses. 💡MUST KNOW: Appositives generally appear on the GMAT in the form of nouns, pronouns, noun phrases, or noun clauses that modify other nouns, pronouns, noun phrases, or noun clauses.

item.title: 5.8.1 Appositives Almost Always Appear Beside What They Modify.
item.description: Lesson Content Appositives usually, though not always, appear beside what they modify. Incorrect: My brother, running through the park, Eduardo saw a rare California condor. The separation of “Eduardo” from what it modifies, “my brother,” results in an awkward, illogical sentence. Running through the park, my brother Eduardo saw a rare California condor. Let’s look at another example: Incorrect: A brown dog, jumping up and down, Brownie was excited to see its owners.

item.title: 5.8.2 Abstract Appositives
item.description: Lesson Content So far, the appositives that we have discussed have renamed and modified specific words or phrases. Consider the following example: Earth’s population of humans is expected to increase substantially over the coming decades, a development that could have significant negative effects on quality of life on the planet. The phrase “a development that could have significant negative effects on quality of life” is an abstract appositive. Let’s consider another example:

item.title: 5.8.3 How Abstract Appositives May Be Used in Sentence Correction Questions
item.description: Lesson Content Choosing the correct answer to a Sentence Correction question generally involves choosing the choice that most effectively conveys a meaning. Consider the following pair of examples: In 1957, Chien-Shiung Wu was passed over by those in charge of awarding of the Nobel Prize in Physics, which many people found outrageous. In 1957, Chien-Shiung Wu was passed over by those in charge of awarding of the Nobel Prize in Physics, a decision that many people found outrageous.

item.title: 5.9 Participles and Participial Phrases
item.description: Lesson Content Sentences containing participles or participial phrases appear quite often in Sentence Correction questions. Let’s begin by discussing present participles and past participles. Present Participles and Past Participles A participle is a verb form that functions as a modifier. Present Participle Examples: “jumping,” “running,” “singing,” and “setting” Past Participle Examples: “ruined,” “laminated,” “spoken,” and “swollen” 💡MUST KNOW: A participle is a verb form that functions as a modifier. Participles come in two varieties: present participles, which always end in -ing, and past participles, which usually end in –ed or -en.

item.title: 5.9.1 A Participle on its Own Can Never Be the Main Verb in a Clause
item.description: Lesson Content A participle can never on its own function as the main verb in a clause. 💡MUST KNOW: A participle can never on its own function as the main verb (finite verb) in a clause. So, a sentence in which the only verb form is a participle will not be complete. Because participles look like verbs, sometimes a participle can be perceived to be the main verb in a sentence when, in fact, no main verb (finite verb) actually exists.

item.title: 5.9.2 Participial Phrases
item.description: Lesson Content In Sentence Correction questions, participles often appear in participial phrases, which are phrases based on, and usually beginning with, participles. Here are some examples of, present participial phrases (phrases based on present participles): spinning incessantly jumping in the grass successfully navigating the channel The following phrases are past participial phrases (phrases based on past participles): ruined by vandals lost in the fog quickly forgotten by many Let’s now discuss how participial phrases are used in sentences.

item.title: 5.9.3 How Present Participial Phrases are Used in Sentences
item.description: Lesson Content For the purposes of learning what we need to know in order to correctly answer GMAT Sentence Correction questions, we can put most present participial phrases, which are phrases based on present participles, or “–ing” verbs, into three general categories: (1) Present participial phrases used to restrictively modify specific nouns. (2) Present participial phrases that appear at the beginning of or in the middle of clauses and modify multiple aspects of the clauses, are nonrestrictive, and can be treated as nonrestrictive noun modifiers for the purpose of placement.

item.title: 5.9.4 Present Participial Phrases that Restrictively Modify Specific Nouns
item.description: Lesson Content A present participial phrase can be used to restrictively modify a specific noun. For example: The cat licking its paws is considering taking a nap. Notice that the present participial phrase “licking its paws” serves to restrictively modify the noun “cat,” making clear to us that the sentence is not speaking of all cats; it’s speaking of a specific cat – the one licking its paws. When a present participial phrase is used to restrictively modify a specific noun, the present participial phrase is placed adjacent to – usually after – the noun that it modifies, without a comma between the phrase and the noun:

item.title: 5.9.5 Present Participial Phrases that Appear at the Beginning or in the Middle of Clauses, are Nonrestrictive, and Can be Treated as Noun Modifiers for the Purpose of Placement
item.description: Lesson Content A present participial phrase that appears at the beginning or in the middle of a clause and is set off from the rest of the clause by a comma or commas can be treated as a nonrestrictive noun modifier. Sailing with the wind, the clipper ship quickly reached its destination. The present participial phrase “sailing with the wind” modifies the noun “clipper ship.” It also explains how the clipper ship reached its destination.

item.title: 5.9.6 Present Participial Phrases that Appear at the Ends of Clauses and Modify Entire Clauses
item.description: Lesson Content When a present participial phrase comes at the end of a clause and is set off from that clause by a comma, the participial phrase does not have to modify the noun closest to it. For example: The horses ran across the field, kicking up dirt with their hooves. Notice that the present participial phrase “kicking up dirt with their hooves” is separated from the clause by a comma.

item.title: 5.9.7 The Meaning Conveyed by a Present Participial Phrase Must Fit with the Rest of the Sentence in which the Phrase Appears
item.description: Lesson Content Present participial phrases always indicate simultaneity. Consider the following sentence: Using fresh ingredients, the chef prepared the meal. Notice that these two things happen at the same time. However, notice what happens in the following two sentences: Incorrect: Using fresh ingredients, the chef looked for a recipe for the meal. Incorrect: Using fresh ingredients, the chef served the meal on white china. Neither of these sentences is logical because both of them use present participial phrases to describe events that would not possibly have occurred simultaneously with the events described by the main clauses.

item.title: 5.9.8 Past Participial Phrases Used to Restrictively Modify Nouns
item.description: Lesson Content Past participial phrases, phrases based on past participles (usually “-ed” or “-en” verbs), can be used to restrictively modify nouns. In these situations, the past participial phrase is placed directly before or after the noun that it modifies and is not separated from the noun by commas. 💡MUST KNOW: Past participial phrases can be used to restrictively modify nouns. In these situations, the past participial phrase is placed directly before or after the nouns that it modifies and is not separated from the noun by commas.

item.title: 5.9.9 Past Participial Phrases Used to Nonrestrictively Modify Nouns
item.description: Lesson Content A past participial phrase can be used to nonrestrictively modify a noun. 💡MUST KNOW: A past participial phrase can be used to nonrestrictively modify a noun. In such a case, the phrase may appear directly before or directly after the noun that it modifies and is separated from the noun and other sentence elements by commas. Consider the following example: At the top of the hill stood a small house, painted white.

item.title: 5.9.10 Issues Involving Past Participial Phrases
item.description: Lesson Content Sentence Correction question creators can create issues in sentences by misplacing past participial phrases. Incorrect: Awarded the Nobel Prize for his work in chemistry, research done by Linus Pauling was wide-ranging and hard-hitting. Notice the opening past participial phrase, “awarded the Nobel Prize for his work in chemistry.” What follows this phrase must be the noun that it is meant to modify: Linus Pauling. Awarded the Nobel Prize for his work in chemistry, Linus Pauling conducted research that was wide-ranging and hard-hitting.

item.title: 5.9.11 Use of a Restrictive or Nonrestrictive Past Participial Phrase where using a Past Participial Phrase of the Other Type, Nonrestrictive or Restrictive, Would Make More Sense
item.description: Lesson Content A second way in which Sentence Correction question writers can create issues involving past participial phrases is to use a restrictive or nonrestrictive past participial phrase where using a past participial phrase of the other type, nonrestrictive or restrictive, would make more sense. Incorrect: Only a tiny percentage of students pursue degrees in physics considered one of the most challenging college subjects. Since there is no comma between “physics” and the past participial phrase “considered one of the …,” the use of the phrase restricts “physics” to one particular type of physics, the physics considered one of the most challenging college subjects.

item.title: 5.10 Absolute Phrases
item.description: Lesson Content An absolute phrase is a phrase that: • consists of only a noun and its modifiers, and • modifies an entire clause. To remember that absolute phrases modify entire clauses, people sometimes say, “Absolute phrases modify absolutely everything.” The following sentence contains an absolute phrase. His work completed, John headed home for the night. In this example, “his work completed” is an absolute phrase. Here’s another: Jennifer sat waiting in the dentist chair, her arms and legs crossed.

item.title: 5.10.1 How Absolute Phrases Can Appear in Sentence Correction Questions
item.description: Lesson Content In some cases, absolute phrases are used in ways that make correct answers clearly better than the other options. Let’s begin by discussing how an absolute phrase can make an answer choice correct by being the best means of expressing a thought. Consider the following two sentence versions. Incorrect: Having spirits, which had been lifted by the joyful music, the crowd sang along with the band. Their spirits having been lifted by the joyful music, the crowd sang along with the band.

item.title: 6.1 Introduction
item.description: Lesson Content Getting Sentence Correction questions correct often requires considering verb tense, mood, or voice. However, as is the case with other aspects of sentence construction, too often people attempt to use their “ear” to answer questions involving these concepts. In the following sections, we’ll cover various verb tense, mood, and voice related concepts and strategies. Let’s begin by discussing the present tense.

item.title: 6.2 The Present Tense
item.description: Lesson Content Despite its name, the present tense is not most commonly used in describing events that are presently occurring. Consider the examples below: The red heart is a universal symbol of love. Sara loves going to the beach. The dogs enjoy running in the woods. Pigs don’t like too much direct sunlight on their sensitive skin. Brazilians celebrate Carnaval every year. Rome is the capital city of Italy. Eating pizza is not healthy.

item.title: 6.3 The Simple Past Tense
item.description: Lesson Content When a simple past tense verb is used, we’re describing an event that occurred – and ended – in the past. 💡MUST KNOW: The simple past tense is used to describe events that occurred – and ended – in the past. The simple past tense is most commonly formed by placing the ending –ed after the stem of a regular verb. He finished his paper just before the deadline.

item.title: 6.4 The Future Tense
item.description: Lesson Content The future tense is used for discussing events that have not yet occurred but will occur in the future. The little boy will go to kindergarten next year. John and Rebecca will be at the movies tonight. Blaze and Molly will eat their dinner a bit later. The rabbit will rest for a few minutes next to the brook. The sun will burn out one day. Notice that each of these examples describes an event that will occur in the future.

item.title: 6.5 Context Clues in Sentences Tell Us Which Tense Must be Used
item.description: Lesson Content Sentence Correction questions involving verb tense issues – even difficult ones – become easy once we realize that each of the sentence versions in these questions will include well-placed clues indicating exactly which tense should be used. 💡MUST KNOW: Correctly answering verb, tense, mood questions is more about solving a puzzle than it is about being a grammarian. Will we need some grammar knowledge? We will. 💡MUST KNOW:

item.title: 6.5.1 VTM Strategy One: Use Verb Context Clues to Eliminate Versions with Tenses that Can’t Work Together
item.description: Lesson Content When faced with an SC question involving verb tense, one powerful strategy that we can utilize is to use verb context clues in the sentence versions to eliminate those with verb tenses that simply can’t work together. For example, consider the following three sentences. Incorrect: Each time I traveled to Colombia, I visit Medellín and Cartagena. Incorrect: Each time will travel to Colombia, I visit Medellín and Cartagena.

item.title: 6.5.2 VTM Strategy Two: Use Other Context Clues to Eliminate Versions with Tenses that Can’t Work
item.description: Lesson Content In addition to verb context clues, GMAT questions will provide us with all sorts of other helpful clues that will allow us to rule out sentence versions with verb tenses that can’t possibly work. Consider the following sentence versions: Incorrect: In the past, Rome’s senate experiences high levels of corruption. Incorrect: In the past, Rome’s senate will experience high levels of corruption. Correct: In the past, Rome’s senate experienced high levels of corruption.

item.title: 6.6 Present Perfect Tense
item.description: Lesson Content When an event began in the past and continues into the present, we use the present perfect tense. 💡MUST KNOW: When an action began in the past and continues into the present, we use the present perfect tense The present perfect tense is formed by combining “has” or “have” with the past participle of the main verb. I have lived here for five years. The above sentence conveys that the event “lived” began in the past but is still happening in the present moment.

item.title: 6.6.1 When We Must Use the Present Perfect Tense
item.description: Lesson Content On the GMAT, when “since” is used to convey that a subject has engaged in an action from the past to the present, the present perfect tense must be used. 💡MUST KNOW: When “since” is used to convey that a subject has engaged in an action from the past to the present, the present perfect tense must be used. Consider the examples below. She has worked with a vocal coach ever since her early years as a singer.

item.title: 6.6.2 Situations in Which the Present Perfect Tense Can’t Be Used
item.description: Lesson Content When a sentence describes an event that has clearly ended in the past, the use of the present perfect is not correct. 💡MUST KNOW: When a sentence describes an event that clearly ended in the past, the present perfect is not correct. The reason is that, as we know, the present perfect tense describes an action that began in the past and continues to be happening in the present or continues to be affecting the present, so if the action ended in the past, using the present perfect is illogical.

item.title: 6.6.3 When the Present Perfect Is Not Mandatory, but Common
item.description: Lesson Content There are a few other words that are clues indicating that the use of the present perfect tense may make sense. For example: Native Americans have lived in what is now the United States for at least the last 15,000 years. Because Ale has lived in Vietnam for more than two years, she is fluent in Vietnamese. Over the last one million years, human civilizations have undergone evolutionary change.

item.title: 6.7 The Past Perfect Tense
item.description: Lesson Content The past perfect tense is used in describing a past action that occurred before another past action. The past perfect tense = “had” + the past participle of a verb. For example, “had been,” ”had eaten,” and “had performed” are all in the past perfect. 💡MUST KNOW: The past perfect tense is used with the action that occurred deeper in the past, and the simple past tense is used with the action that occurred in the more recent past.

item.title: 6.7.1 Eliminate Sentences that Reverse the Correct Position of the Past Perfect Tense
item.description: Lesson Content SC questions may try to trick us into choosing wrong answers that reverse the correct placement of the past perfect tense. 💡MUST KNOW: SC questions may try to trick us into choosing wrong answers that reverse the correct placement of the past perfect tense. These questions, which have two past actions mentioned, utilize the past perfect tense with the more recent past action. For example: Incorrect: She had skipped the popcorn at the movies because she ate a big supper.

item.title: 6.7.2 Use the Past Perfect When Describing an Action/Event Completed by a Certain Time in the Past.
item.description: Lesson Content A sentence must use the past perfect tense when the sentence is about an action or event completed by a specified date or time period in the past. For example: By late evening on Election Day, it had become clear that the upstart candidate would soon be the president of the country. Late evening on Election Day is a time in the past, and we have an event, becoming, that was completed before that time.

item.title: 6.7.3 The Past Perfect Tense Is Unnecessary When the Time Sequence Is Clear Without It.
item.description: Lesson Content At this point, you know that, when we have two past actions with one of the actions occurring deeper in the past, we can use the past perfect tense to make the time sequence clear. However, if a sentence makes the sequence of events clear without the use of the past perfect tense, there is no need to use the past perfect. 💡MUST KNOW: If a sentence makes clear the time sequence of past events without the use of the past perfect tense, the use of the past perfect tense is not necessary.

item.title: 6.8 The Future Perfect Tense
item.description: Lesson Content The future perfect tense indicates that an event that has not yet happened will be completed before another event that has not yet happened or before a specific time in the future. 💡MUST KNOW: The future perfect tense indicates that an event that has not yet happened will be completed before another event that has not yet happened or before a specific time in the future. The future perfect tense is formed by combining “will,” the helping verb “have,” and the past participle of the main verb.

item.title: 6.9 The Future in the Past
item.description: Lesson Content The future in the past is used when sentences about past events refer to events that were in the future when the past events occurred. Consider the following example: At the rally, the candidate said that he would take campaign donations only from individuals. The sentence conveys that, at a time in the past, the candidate said that he was going to do something in the future, “take campaign donations only from individuals.

item.title: 6.10 The Continuous Tenses
item.description: Lesson Content The continuous tenses, sometimes called “the progressive tenses,” are used in discussing events that are continuous. 💡MUST KNOW: We use the progressive tenses to discuss actions that are, were, or will be in progress. The purpose of these tenses is to bring the reader’s attention to the temporarily ongoing nature of an action. 💡MUST KNOW: The purpose of the continuous tenses is to bring the reader’s attention to the temporarily ongoing nature of an action.

item.title: 6.10.1 The Present Continuous Tense
item.description: Lesson Content The present continuous tense is used for describing events that are temporarily ongoing in the present. The children are playing outside. The dog is running on the beach. The sun is setting over the Pacific Ocean. The members are taking their placement tests. Notice that, in each of the above sentences, the event is occurring “right now.” In addition, the events are continuous. 💡MUST KNOW: The present continuous tense is used to describe events that are temporarily ongoing in the present.

item.title: 6.10.2 The Past Continuous Tense
item.description: Lesson Content The past continuous tense is used to describe an action or event that was ongoing but has ended. The children were playing outside yesterday.
